message.content:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office CLI - quote and claim operations for magical item insurance
Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_23-17-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7/src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_23-17-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7/src/claim-office.ts
Requirements: See /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_23-17-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7/prompt.md for the full specification including:
- Item values and base premiums (sword, amulet, staff, potion, components)
- Block discount for 3 alike components
- Premium modifiers (cursed, high enchantment, loyalty, first insurance, follow-up contract, processing fee)
- Rounding in MHPCO's favor (premium up, payout down)
- Claim processing with deductible, cap, dragon material, high enchantment clause
- Multi-step scenarios with customer state
- Error cases (unknown type, mismatch, negative amount)
- CLI reads JSON from stdin, writes to stdout

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

content.text: I'll start by reading the specification thoroughly, then create the example mapping file and test list.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### Schema example
237	
238	Stdin:
239	
240	```json
241	{
242	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
243	  "steps": [
244	    {
245	      "op": "quote",
246	      "items": [
247	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
248	      ]
249	    },
250	    {
251	      "op": "claim",
252	      "policy": 0,
253	      "incident": {
254	        "cause": "fire",
255	        "damages": [
256	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
257	        ]
258	      }
259	    }
260	  ]
261	}
262	```
263	
264	Stdout shape:
265	
266	```json
267	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
268	```
269	
270	## Expected Output Files
271	
272	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
273	- Test files
274	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
275	
276	## Constraints
277	
278	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
279	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
280	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
281	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
282

input.content:
# Claim Office — Example Mapping

This document is the shared spec memory for the TDD subagents. It distills
`prompt.md` into rules, examples, open questions, and the rationale for each
`it.todo()` test in `src/claim-office.spec.ts`.

The implementation is a CLI (`src/cli.ts`) that reads a JSON scenario from
stdin and writes a JSON `{results: [...]}` document to stdout. The library
module `src/claim-office.ts` contains the pure logic; tests typically exercise
that pure logic directly, with one or two end-to-end tests through the CLI
(stdin/stdout) for the error-exit / unknown-type behaviour that is described
only in terms of CLI exit status.

## Rules

### R1 — Item insurance values and base premiums

Each main item has a fixed insurance value and base premium:

- Sword: 1000 G / 100 G
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components (runes, moonstones, ...) are 250 G value / 25 G base premium each.

### R2 — Block discount for components

A "building block" of exactly 3 alike components has a special base premium
of 60 G (instead of 3×25 = 75 G). The block requires exactly 3 of the same
type — different component types do not combine.

### R3 — Premium modifiers

- **Cursed** items: +50 % surcharge on that item's base premium.
- **High enchantment** (level ≥ 5): +30 % surcharge on that item's base
  premium.
- **Loyalty** (customer ≥ 2 years with MHPCO): −20 % discount on policy base
  premium.
- **First insurance**: +10 % surcharge on policy base premium. Applied
  per-quote regardless of customer history (each item in a quote is treated
  as a first insurance for that item).
- **Follow-up contract**: −15 % discount on policy base premium, applied to
  every contract after the customer's first in the scenario.
- **Processing fee**: flat +5 G added at the very end.

Modifier scope:
- Item-specific (cursed, high enchantment) → percentage of the affected item's
  base premium only.
- Policy-wide (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up) → percentage of the policy
  base premium (sum of item base premiums).
- Processing fee added last.

### R4 — Rounding

All intermediate amounts are kept as exact fractions. Only the final premium
or payout is rounded — premium UP, payout DOWN (always in MHPCO's favor).

### R5 — Claim processing

- Insurance sum = sum of items' insurance values (unmodified by premium
  modifiers and not affected by block discount).
- Cap = 2 × insurance sum, per policy. Successive claims deplete the cap.
- 100 G deductible applies once per damage entry (per damaged item).
- High-enchantment clause (item enchantment ≥ 8): damage reimbursed at 50 %
  before the deductible.
- Dragon material: full reimbursement (before deductible).
- If both apply (dragon AND enchantment ≥ 8), the 50 % clause wins.

### R6 — CLI / scenario semantics

- Input: `{customer, steps[]}` with `customer.yearsWithMHPCO` and steps that
  are either `{op: "quote", items: [...]}` or
  `{op: "claim", policy: <step-index>, incident: {cause, damages: [...]}}`.
- Output: `{results: [...]}` aligned with steps; quote → `{premium}`,
  claim → `{payout, remainingCap}`.
- Customer state across steps: each successive quote in the same scenario
  is a follow-up contract (so the very first quote is the customer's first
  contract; loyalty depends solely on `yearsWithMHPCO`).
- Errors (unknown type, claim mismatch, negative damage amount): CLI exits
  non-zero, writes description to stderr, no `results` to stdout.

## Examples (mapped to tests)

### E1 — Empty item list → 5 G (only fee)
### E2 — Base premiums of single main items
- 1 sword (plain, customer 0y, first contract): base 100 + first 10 + fee 5 = 115 G
- (We use a similar minimal case to assert each main item type)

### E3 — Component base premium
- 1 rune (250 G value, 25 G base): premium = 25 + 2.5 (first) + 5 = 32.5 → rounded up to 33 G

### E4 — Block discount (3 alike components)
- 2 runes → base 50 G
- 3 runes → base 60 G (block)
- 4 runes → base 100 G (block + 1 lone = 60 + 25)
- 7 runes → base 175 G (2 blocks + 1 lone = 60 + 60 + 25 = 145? — spec says 175)
  - Reading spec carefully: 7 runes → 175 G. That implies the rule is:
    one block of 3 at 60 G, the remaining 4 priced individually at 25 G:
    60 + 4×25 = 160 G — NOT 175 G. Re-checking: actually 175 G = 7×25,
    i.e. NO block applies for 7. The spec phrase "block applies when
    exactly 3" means only 3 alike → use block; otherwise individual.
    So 4 runes → 4×25 = 100 G (matches spec), 7 runes → 7×25 = 175 G
    (matches spec). The block applies ONLY when the count of an alike
    component group is exactly 3.

### E5 — Alike components are same TYPE (❓ resolved)
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (3×25; no block — different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (60 + 60 — two separate blocks)

### E6 — Cursed-item scope (❓ resolved)
- Policy: cursed sword + plain amulet → base 160 G; cursed adds 50 G
  (50 % of 100, not of 160) → 210 G before policy modifiers and fee.

### E7 — Modifier thresholds
- yearsWithMHPCO = 2 → loyalty applies.
- enchantment = 5 → high-enchantment surcharge applies.
- enchantment = 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge.
- enchantment = 8 with dragon material, damage 1000 → payout 400 G
  (50 % clause then deductible).

### E8 — Newcomer with cursed sword (integration)
- customer 0y, 1st contract; cursed steel sword enchantment 3
- = 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first = 160 + 5 fee = 165 G

### E9 — Long-standing customer's second contract (integration)
- customer 3y; 2nd quote in scenario; cursed steel sword enchantment 7
- = 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench − 20 loyalty + 10 first
  − 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee = 160 G

### E10 — Rounding
- premium 197.5 → 198 G (up)
- payout 350.5 → 350 G (down)

### E11 — Deductible per damage event
- dragon attack damages sword (500) + amulet (300) → payout 600 G
  ((500−100) + (300−100))

### E12 — Standard reimbursement
- steel sword, enchantment 3, damage 500 → payout 400 G
- rune (no enchantment/material), damage 200 → payout 100 G

### E13 — Enchantment threshold vs dragon material
- dragon sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (50 % wins)
- dragon sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 → payout 700 G (dragon, no 50 %)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (50 % only)

### E14 — Multiple items of same type (❓ resolved)
- Policy with 2 swords → insurance sum 2000, cap 4000.
- Dragon attack damages both swords (2 entries in damages) → each entry
  has its own deductible.
- More damage entries of a type than insured items → CLI exits non-zero.

### E15 — Cap exhaustion
- Sword + amulet → insurance sum 1600, cap 3200.
- Cursed sword (1000 value, premium with mods 165) → cap 2000
  (unmodified value).
- Sword + 3 runes (block) → insurance sum 1750 (block discount only
  affects premium, not insurance sum).
- Sword (cap 2000), two claims of 1500 each:
  - claim 1 → payout 1400, remainingCap 600
  - claim 2 → payout 600, remainingCap 0 (desired 1400 truncated by cap)

### E16 — Error cases
- Unknown item type in quote → CLI non-zero exit, error on stderr, no
  results on stdout.
- Claim references item not in policy (e.g. amulet damage when only sword
  insured, or unknown type) → non-zero exit, stderr error.
- Damage amount −200 → non-zero exit, stderr error.

## Questions / Clarifications

- ❓ "Alike" components → resolved (same TYPE, e.g. runes with runes only).
- ❓ Cursed surcharge scope on multi-item policies → resolved (item-specific).
- ❓ Multiple items of same type → resolved (allowed; each damage entry has
  its own deductible; mismatch → reject claim).
- ❓ "First insurance" surcharge → resolved (applies per item in every quote,
  regardless of customer history).

## Per-test rationale

Tests live in `src/claim-office.spec.ts` and exercise the pure library
function (let's call it `runScenario(input)` returning `{results}`). End-to-end
CLI/stderr tests are deferred to where the spec demands process exit codes
(error cases).

Ordered simple → complex:

1. Empty items quote → 5 G (R1, R3 fee, E1).
2. Single sword (newcomer, plain) → 115 G (R1 + first insurance + fee).
3. Single amulet → 71 G (60 + 6 first + 5 fee = 71).
4. Single staff → 93 G (80 + 8 + 5).
5. Single potion → 49 G (40 + 4 + 5).
6. Single rune → 33 G (25 + 2.5 first → 27.5, +5 fee = 32.5 → round up 33).
7. 2 runes → no block, base 50 G; premium 50 + 5 + 5 = 60 G.
8. 3 runes (block) → base 60 G; premium 60 + 6 + 5 = 71 G.
9. 4 runes → 4×25 = 100 G base; premium 100 + 10 + 5 = 115 G.
10. 7 runes → 7×25 = 175 G base; premium 175 + 17.5 + 5 = 197.5 → 198 G
    (also covers rounding-up rule E10).
11. 2 runes + 1 moonstone → base 75 G (no block — different types).
12. 3 runes + 3 moonstones → base 120 G (two separate blocks).
13. Cursed steel sword newcomer (E8) → 165 G.
14. Cursed sword + plain amulet (E6) → base 160 + 50 curse = 210 before
    policy mods/fee. Newcomer, first contract → +10 % first =
    210 + 21 = 231 + 5 = 236 G. (Sanity check: 10 % first applies to
    policy base 160, not to the post-curse 210? Per R3, first insurance
    is policy-wide on the policy BASE premium (sum of item base premiums),
    not on the curse-augmented amount. So: 160 base → +16 first → 176;
    + 50 curse (item-specific, on base) = 226; + 5 fee = 231 G.
    Re-reading: spec says "policy-wide modifiers apply to the policy base
    premium (the sum of all item base premiums)". So curse (item) and
    first (policy on base sum) are both percentages of base values. We
    use additive composition: total = sum_base + item_surcharges_on_base
    + policy_modifiers_on_sum_base + fee. For E8 newcomer with cursed
    sword: 100 + 50 (curse on 100) + 10 (first on 100) + 5 = 165 ✓.
    For E6 cursed sword + amulet, newcomer first: 160 + 50 (curse on 100)
    + 16 (first on 160) + 5 = 231 G.
15. Enchantment threshold: steel sword enchantment 5 → high-ench applies
    → 100 + 30 + 10 first + 5 fee = 145 G.
16. Enchantment threshold: steel sword enchantment 4 → no high-ench
    → 100 + 10 first + 5 fee = 115 G.
17. Enchantment + cursed combine: cursed sword enchantment 5 →
    100 + 50 + 30 + 10 + 5 = 195 G.
18. Loyalty exactly at 2 years: customer 2y, plain sword (first contract)
    → 100 − 20 loyalty + 10 first + 5 fee = 95 G.
19. Long-standing 2nd contract integration (E9) → 160 G.
20. Rounding premium up: case 10 already covers it. Add a payout-down case
    via claim test (#27 below).
21. Standard claim: steel sword enchantment 3, damage 500 → payout 400 G,
    remainingCap = 2000 − 400 = 1600 G.
22. Standard claim on rune: damage 200 → payout 100 G; cap = 2×250 = 500.
23. High-enchantment clause: steel sword enchantment 9, damage 1000 →
    payout 400 G (500 − 100).
24. Dragon material clause: dragon sword enchantment 5, damage 800 →
    payout 700 G (800 − 100).
25. Both clauses: dragon sword enchantment 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G
    (50 % wins, then 500 − 100).
26. Enchantment exactly 8: dragon sword enchantment 8, damage 1000 →
    payout 400 G (E7 threshold).
27. Payout rounding down: a damage of 801 on a dragon sword enchantment 9
    → 50 % = 400.5, − 100 = 300.5 → round down 300 G.
28. Deductible per damage event: dragon attack damages sword (500) +
    amulet (300) → payout 600 G; remainingCap = 2×(1000+600) − 600 =
    2600 G.
29. Multiple same-type items: two swords, insurance sum 2000, cap 4000;
    one dragon attack damages both swords (two entries) →
    payout (500−100)+(500−100) = 800 G, remainingCap 3200 G.
30. Cap exhaustion across claims: single sword (cap 2000); two successive
    1500 G claims → first {payout 1400, remainingCap 600},
    second {payout 600, remainingCap 0}.
31. Insurance sum with block: sword + 3 runes → insurance sum 1750
    (block does not change insurance sum); cap 3500. Verify via a claim:
    e.g. damage to sword 200 → payout 100, remainingCap 3400.
32. Error: unknown item type in quote → CLI non-zero exit; no results on
    stdout; stderr non-empty.
33. Error: claim references item not in policy → CLI non-zero exit;
    stderr non-empty.
34. Error: damage amount negative (−200) → CLI non-zero exit; stderr
    non-empty.
35. Error: more damages of a type than insured items (two sword damages
    when only one sword insured) → CLI non-zero exit.

The CLI-error tests (32–35) are run end-to-end by spawning `node` / `tsx`
on `src/cli.ts`. The pure tests (1–31) call `runScenario(input)` directly.

content.text:
Now let me reconsider one detail more carefully. The spec example E14 (cursed sword + plain amulet) says "210 G before further modifiers and fee" — this confirms my interpretation: curse adds 50 G (50 % of 100). So base 160 + curse 50 = 210 before other modifiers. Then policy-wide modifiers like first insurance apply to "the policy base premium (the sum of all item base premiums)" = 160. So for a newcomer first contract: 160 (sum base) + 50 (curse on 100) + 16 (10 % first on 160) + 5 fee = 231 G. Good.

Let me also reconsider test #14: I had two competing values. The spec only states the value 210 G BEFORE further modifiers and fee. The newcomer first-contract case is my synthesis. Let me adjust to match an example actually stated. I'll keep test 14 as a direct check of the partial total at 210 G, but it's hard to test that without exposing an intermediate. Better: drop the newcomer-cursed-sword+amulet exact total, since the spec doesn't pin down a final value. I'll keep just enough tests to cover all the rules and examples from the spec.

Let me now write the test file.

The test list is complete. 36 `it.todo()` tests, ordered simple → complex, covering every rule, example, and clarifying question (❓) in the spec.

## Test List Created

**Feature**: MHPCO Claim Office — quote and claim operations
**Test file**: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_23-17-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7/src/claim-office.spec.ts
**Example mapping**: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_23-17-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7/example-mapping/claim-office.md
**Tests**: 36

**Test groups (simple → complex):**

1. Empty quote → fee only (1 test)
2. Single main item base premiums (4 tests: sword, amulet, staff, potion)
3. Component pricing & block discount: 1, 2, 3, 4, 7 runes (5 tests; the 7-runes case also exercises premium rounding-up)
4. Alike-component scope (❓ resolved: same type only) — 2 tests
5. Item-specific modifiers: cursed, high-enchantment, threshold at 5, threshold at 4, combined (4 tests; cursed-sword integration example included)
6. Multi-item modifier scope (❓ resolved: item-specific on item, policy-wide on sum) — 1 test
7. Policy-wide modifiers: loyalty threshold, long-standing 2nd contract integration (2 tests)
8. Claims — standard, high-enchantment, dragon material, both clauses, threshold at 8, payout rounding-down (7 tests)
9. Deductible per damage event (1 test)
10. Multiple same-type items (1 test)
11. Cap exhaustion across claims (1 test)
12. Block discount does not change insurance sum (1 test)
13. Cap based on unmodified value, not modified premium (1 test)
14. CLI error cases: unknown type, claim mismatch, negative amount, more damages than items (4 tests)

The implementation module will expose `runScenario(input)`. Error cases will be tested end-to-end against `src/cli.ts` (exit code + stderr).
